news_summary: Scientists have discovered a giant magnetic twist within the Milky Way, revealing new insights into our galaxy's magnetic structure.
news_summary_fr: Des scientifiques ont découvert une torsion magnétique géante au sein de la Voie lactée, apportant ainsi un nouvel éclairage sur la structure magnétique de notre galaxie.
news_summary_es: Los científicos han descubierto una enorme espiral magnética en el interior de la Vía Láctea, lo que ha aportado nuevos conocimientos sobre la estructura magnética de nuestra galaxia.

articles.title: Scientists found a giant magnetic “twist” hidden inside the Milky Way
articles.summary: Astronomers have uncovered a strange magnetic “flip” hidden inside the Milky Way. Using a new radio telescope, researchers mapped the galaxy’s magnetic field in unprecedented detail and discovered that a mysterious reversal in the Sagittarius Arm cuts diagonally across space. The finding could reshape how scientists understand the structure and future evolution of our galaxy.
articles.content:
For hundreds of years, astronomers have studied the night sky in an effort to understand the forces shaping the universe. One of the most important, yet invisible, forces inside the Milky Way is its magnetic field. Now, researchers at the University of Calgary are producing one of the clearest views yet of that hidden structure.

"Without a magnetic field, the galaxy would collapse in on itself due to gravity," says Brown, a professor in the Department of Physics and Astronomy at the University of Calgary.

"We need to know what the magnetic field of the galaxy looks like now, so we can create accurate models that predict how it will evolve."

This month, Brown and her collaborators published two studies in The Astrophysical Journal and The Astrophysical Journal Supplement Series. Their work introduces a major new dataset that astronomers around the world will be able to use, along with a new model explaining how the Milky Way's magnetic field may have changed over time.

Mapping the Milky Way's Magnetic Field

To carry out the research, the team relied on a new telescope at the Dominion Radio Astrophysical Observatory in British Columbia, a facility operated by the National Research Council Canada. The instrument allowed scientists to survey the northern sky across a wide range of radio frequencies.

"The broad coverage really lets you get at the details about the magnetic field structure," says Dr. Anna Ordog, PhD, lead author of the first study.

The observations became part of the Global Magneto-Ionic Medium Survey (GMIMS), an international effort focused on mapping the Milky Way's magnetic field in unprecedented detail. The result is a high quality dataset that provides scientists with a much clearer picture of the galaxy's invisible magnetic environment.

Tracking Faraday Rotation in Space

The researchers collected the data by measuring an effect called Faraday rotation, which occurs when radio waves travel through areas filled with electrons and magnetic fields.

"You can think of it like refraction. A straw in a glass of water looks bent because of how light interacts with matter," says Rebecca Booth, a PhD candidate working with Brown and lead author of the second study. "Faraday rotation is a similar concept, but it's electrons and magnetic fields in space interacting with radio waves."

By analyzing these changes in radio waves, the team was able to trace hidden magnetic structures throughout the galaxy.

A Strange Magnetic Reversal in the Sagittarius Arm

One of the most surprising discoveries involved the Sagittarius Arm, a region of the Milky Way where the magnetic field appears to reverse direction.

"If you could look at the galaxy from above, the overall magnetic field is going clockwise," says Brown. "But, in the Sagittarius Arm, it's going counterclockwise. We didn't understand how the transition occurred. Then one day, Anna brought in some data, and I went, 'O.M.G., the reversal's diagonal!'"

Booth later expanded on Ordog's finding using the newly collected data.

"My work presents a new three-dimensional model for the magnetic field reversal. From Earth, this would appear as the diagonal that we observe in the data," Booth explains.

The discovery provides researchers with an important new clue about the hidden magnetic architecture of the Milky Way and could help scientists better understand how galaxies evolve over time.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about the Milky Way's magnetic field, made possible by advanced radio telescope observations. This discovery enhances understanding of galactic structure and evolution, providing a meaningful contribution to space science with broad implications for astronomy.
articles.personality_title: Scientists discover a giant magnetic twist inside the Milky Way
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For a long time, astronomers have studied the Milky Way to understand its hidden forces. One important but invisible force is the galaxy’s magnetic field, which helps keep the galaxy stable and stops it from collapsing under gravity.

**What happened** – Using a new radio telescope in Canada, scientists from the University of Calgary mapped the Milky Way’s magnetic field in great detail. They found a surprising “flip” or reversal in the magnetic field inside a section called the Sagittarius Arm. This reversal runs diagonally across space, something not seen before.

**Impact** – This discovery changes how scientists think about the Milky Way’s magnetic structure. Knowing the exact shape and behavior of the magnetic field helps researchers build better models to predict how our galaxy might change in the future. It also gives clues about how galaxies grow and evolve.

**What’s next step** – The new detailed data will be shared worldwide, allowing other astronomers to study the Milky Way’s magnetic field more closely. Scientists will use this information to improve their understanding of galactic magnetic fields and explore how these forces affect stars and space over time.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Using new radio telescope data, scientists found a surprising diagonal magnetic flip inside the Milky Way’s Sagittarius Arm, offering fresh insight into our galaxy’s hidden structure and future.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent une torsion magnétique géante dans la Voie lactée
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis longtemps, les astronomes étudient la Voie lactée pour comprendre ses forces cachées. L’une des forces importantes mais invisibles est le champ magnétique de la galaxie, qui aide à la stabiliser et l’empêche de s’effondrer sous la gravité.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Grâce à un nouveau radiotélescope au Canada, des chercheurs de l’Université de Calgary ont cartographié le champ magnétique de la Voie lactée en détail. Ils ont découvert un renversement surprenant du champ magnétique dans une région appelée le Bras du Sagittaire. Ce renversement se trouve en diagonale dans l’espace, ce qui n’avait jamais été vu auparavant.

**Impact** – Cette découverte change la façon dont les scientifiques comprennent la structure magnétique de la Voie lactée. Connaître la forme exacte et le comportement du champ magnétique aide à créer de meilleurs modèles pour prévoir l’évolution future de notre galaxie. Cela donne aussi des indices sur la croissance et l’évolution des galaxies.

**Prochaine étape** – Les nouvelles données détaillées seront partagées dans le monde entier, permettant à d’autres astronomes d’étudier plus précisément le champ magnétique de la Voie lactée. Les chercheurs utiliseront ces informations pour mieux comprendre les champs magnétiques galactiques et leur effet sur les étoiles et l’espace avec le temps.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Grâce à des données de radiotélescope, les scientifiques ont trouvé un renversement magnétique diagonal surprenant dans le Bras du Sagittaire de la Voie lactée, offrant de nouvelles clés sur la structure cachée et l’avenir de notre galaxie.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren un giro magnético gigante dentro de la Vía Láctea
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante mucho tiempo, los astrónomos han estudiado la Vía Láctea para entender sus fuerzas ocultas. Una fuerza importante pero invisible es el campo magnético de la galaxia, que ayuda a mantenerla estable y evita que se colapse por la gravedad.

**Qué pasó** – Usando un nuevo radiotelescopio en Canadá, científicos de la Universidad de Calgary mapearon el campo magnético de la Vía Láctea con gran detalle. Encontraron una sorprendente “inversión” o cambio en el campo magnético dentro de una zona llamada el Brazo de Sagitario. Esta inversión cruza el espacio en diagonal, algo nunca visto antes.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento cambia la forma en que los científicos entienden la estructura magnética de la Vía Láctea. Conocer la forma exacta y el comportamiento del campo magnético ayuda a crear mejores modelos para predecir cómo podría cambiar nuestra galaxia en el futuro. También da pistas sobre cómo crecen y evolucionan las galaxias.

**Próximo paso** – Los nuevos datos detallados serán compartidos en todo el mundo, permitiendo que otros astrónomos estudien con más precisión el campo magnético de la Vía Láctea. Los científicos usarán esta información para mejorar su comprensión de los campos magnéticos galácticos y explorar cómo estas fuerzas afectan a las estrellas y al espacio con el tiempo.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Usando nuevos datos de radiotelescopio, científicos encontraron una sorprendente inversión magnética diagonal dentro del Brazo de Sagitario de la Vía Láctea, ofreciendo una nueva visión sobre la estructura oculta y el futuro de nuestra galaxia.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vast spiral galaxy viewed from above, its swirling arms glowing softly in natural blues and gentle golds, with a prominent diagonal magnetic “twist” shown as flowing, ribbon-like lines of light weaving counterclockwise through one spiral arm against the overall clockwise magnetic flow, symbolizing the hidden, dynamic magnetic field shaping the Milky Way.
